topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I kept my gaze fixed on the blank screen of my laptop, the cursor blinking mockingly, as if daring me to reveal the deception. Every time a footfall echoed in the quiet aisle, my shoulders would tense, pulling my collar up around my neck. My hands, usually steady when typing, felt clammy, the skin prickling with a strange, internal heat. When Professor Albright finally called my name, the sound felt amplified, each syllable landing like a physical blow. I stood up too quickly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum, and my mouth went instantly dry. I could feel the blood rushing to my face, a hot flush creeping up my neck. "Please, Professor," I managed, my voice coming out thin and reedy, "I assure you, this isn't—"

[story 2]

I re-read the accusation three times, the words swimming in my vision like distorted water. The air in the faculty lounge felt thick and suffocating, heavy with unspoken judgment. I kept picking at a loose thread on my sweater, my fingers tracing the rough fibers with obsessive focus. When the department head leaned in, his expression unreadable, I noticed the fine tremor starting in my left hand. It wasn't a shaking from fear, exactly; it was a deep, unsettling stillness, like a machine slowing down just before a critical failure. "We need to discuss the source material," he stated, his tone flat, devoid of warmth. I swallowed hard, the movement catching in my throat. I looked around the room, trying to find an escape route, but every exit seemed blocked by the sheer weight of their scrutiny. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, and I could feel the pressure building behind my sternum, making it difficult to draw a full, steady breath.

[story 3]

The printed document lay on my desk, stark white and damning under the harsh desk lamp. I didn't touch it. Instead, I stared at the reflection of my own strained face in the darkened monitor. My heart was beating an erratic rhythm against my ribs, a frantic drumming I couldn't silence. Every small sound—the distant chatter from the hallway, the scratch of my pen on the notepad—seemed magnified, invasive. I shifted in my seat, the cheap plastic of my chair groaning in protest. When the committee members began their questioning, their voices a low, continuous drone, I found myself focusing intensely on the pattern of the wood grain on my desk. It was a pointless exercise, a desperate attempt to anchor myself to something solid while the ground beneath me felt suddenly unstable, ready to give way. I kept my eyes downcast, willing myself to become invisible, a shadow trying to dissolve into the corner of the room.
